As the market continues to evolve, both landlords and tenants may need to stay informed about these shifting dynamics. The interplay between regulatory changes, economic pressures and local market conditions appears set to shape the rental landscape throughout 2026.

item.title: Software integration offers property managers live repair updates
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property managers and letting agents could benefit from streamlined repair tracking following a new integration between maintenance platform Fixflo and contractor management system BigChange. The partnership aims to reduce administrative burdens by automating updates across both platforms, providing real-time visibility of repair progress from initial instruction through to final invoicing.

How the integration works

The connection between the two systems automatically shares key information without requiring manual input. When contractors using BigChange schedule or reschedule appointments, these changes immediately reflect in Fixflo's platform. Property professionals can monitor job progress without needing to chase contractors for updates, while tenants receive automatic notifications about upcoming visits.

The integration also handles financial aspects of repair work, with completed job dates, invoices and costs transferring seamlessly between systems. The entire workflow – including quotes, approvals and requests for additional work – becomes visible within a single interface. According to the companies, this creates a complete audit trail for each repair job while reducing opportunities for errors or miscommunication.

Potential benefits for property professionals

Dean Falvey, head of contractor operations at Fixflo, explained that much of the frustration in property repairs stems from coordination rather than the actual work: "The contractor works in their own system, and the agent sees it all update in Fixflo." This real-time synchronisation could help property managers by:

  • Eliminating the need to manually request updates from contractors
  • Providing tenants with accurate appointment information
  • Automating invoice reconciliation between systems
  • Creating a transparent record of all repair activities

The integration is currently available to customers using both platforms, potentially offering a solution to one of the most time-consuming aspects of property management. While the technology won't speed up actual repair work, it may significantly reduce the administrative overhead surrounding maintenance issues.

For property professionals juggling multiple repair jobs across different properties, such automated tracking systems could prove invaluable in maintaining tenant satisfaction while keeping operational costs under control. As property management becomes increasingly digitised, integrations like this may become standard practice in the industry.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property managers and letting agents could benefit from streamlined repair tracking following a new integration between maintenance platform Fixflo and contractor management system BigChange. The partnership aims to reduce administrative burdens by automating updates across both platforms, providing real-time visibility of repair progress from initial instruction through to final invoicing.

How the integration works

The connection between the two systems automatically shares key information without requiring manual input. When contractors using BigChange schedule or reschedule appointments, these changes immediately reflect in Fixflo's platform. Property professionals can monitor job progress without needing to chase contractors for updates, while tenants receive automatic notifications about upcoming visits.

The integration also handles financial aspects of repair work, with completed job dates, invoices and costs transferring seamlessly between systems. The entire workflow – including quotes, approvals and requests for additional work – becomes visible within a single interface. According to the companies, this creates a complete audit trail for each repair job while reducing opportunities for errors or miscommunication.

Potential benefits for property professionals

Dean Falvey, head of contractor operations at Fixflo, explained that much of the frustration in property repairs stems from coordination rather than the actual work: "The contractor works in their own system, and the agent sees it all update in Fixflo." This real-time synchronisation could help property managers by:

  • Eliminating the need to manually request updates from contractors
  • Providing tenants with accurate appointment information
  • Automating invoice reconciliation between systems
  • Creating a transparent record of all repair activities

The integration is currently available to customers using both platforms, potentially offering a solution to one of the most time-consuming aspects of property management. While the technology won't speed up actual repair work, it may significantly reduce the administrative overhead surrounding maintenance issues.

For property professionals juggling multiple repair jobs across different properties, such automated tracking systems could prove invaluable in maintaining tenant satisfaction while keeping operational costs under control. As property management becomes increasingly digitised, integrations like this may become standard practice in the industry.

item.title: Asking prices drop 1% in July
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has seen a slight dip in asking prices this July, with a 1% decrease reported by Rightmove. The property portal attributes this decline to a combination of factors, including soaring summer temperatures and the distraction of major sporting events like the World Cup. Whilst this may raise questions for potential sellers, it could present opportunities for buyers looking to enter the market.

What’s Behind the Drop?

Rightmove’s latest data suggests that the summer heatwave and the excitement surrounding the World Cup have contributed to a temporary slowdown in buyer activity. Historically, the summer months often see a lull in property transactions as people prioritise holidays and outdoor activities over house hunting. This year, the added distraction of global football has further dampened demand, leading to a modest reduction in asking prices.

Experts note that such seasonal fluctuations are not uncommon and often balance out over the course of the year. However, homeowners considering selling might want to take these trends into account when planning their next steps. Pricing strategies could play a crucial role in attracting interest during quieter periods.

Opportunities for Buyers

For prospective buyers, the dip in asking prices could be a chance to secure a property at a more favourable rate. With fewer buyers actively searching during this period, competition may be reduced, potentially giving those in the market an edge in negotiations. It could also be worth exploring properties that have been on the market for a while, as sellers may be more open to offers.

That said, it’s important to approach any purchase with caution. Whilst lower asking prices might seem appealing, buyers should ensure they’re making informed decisions based on thorough research and affordability checks. Consulting with a mortgage advisor could help clarify options and ensure financial readiness.

As the summer draws to a close, the housing market is likely to regain momentum. Whether you’re buying or selling, staying informed about market trends and seeking professional guidance could help navigate the ever-changing property landscape with confidence.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has seen a slight dip in asking prices this July, with a 1% decrease reported by Rightmove. The property portal attributes this decline to a combination of factors, including soaring summer temperatures and the distraction of major sporting events like the World Cup. Whilst this may raise questions for potential sellers, it could present opportunities for buyers looking to enter the market.

What’s Behind the Drop?

Rightmove’s latest data suggests that the summer heatwave and the excitement surrounding the World Cup have contributed to a temporary slowdown in buyer activity. Historically, the summer months often see a lull in property transactions as people prioritise holidays and outdoor activities over house hunting. This year, the added distraction of global football has further dampened demand, leading to a modest reduction in asking prices.

Experts note that such seasonal fluctuations are not uncommon and often balance out over the course of the year. However, homeowners considering selling might want to take these trends into account when planning their next steps. Pricing strategies could play a crucial role in attracting interest during quieter periods.

Opportunities for Buyers

For prospective buyers, the dip in asking prices could be a chance to secure a property at a more favourable rate. With fewer buyers actively searching during this period, competition may be reduced, potentially giving those in the market an edge in negotiations. It could also be worth exploring properties that have been on the market for a while, as sellers may be more open to offers.

That said, it’s important to approach any purchase with caution. Whilst lower asking prices might seem appealing, buyers should ensure they’re making informed decisions based on thorough research and affordability checks. Consulting with a mortgage advisor could help clarify options and ensure financial readiness.

As the summer draws to a close, the housing market is likely to regain momentum. Whether you’re buying or selling, staying informed about market trends and seeking professional guidance could help navigate the ever-changing property landscape with confidence.

item.title: BTL figures show ‘incredible resilience’
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The buy-to-let (BTL) market continues to demonstrate remarkable strength, with recent figures highlighting increased lending activity, robust yields, and low levels of arrears. These trends suggest sustained confidence among property investors, despite ongoing economic challenges and regulatory changes in the sector.

Growing Lending Activity

Recent data indicates a notable rise in BTL lending, reflecting renewed interest from both new and experienced landlords. This uptick in borrowing could be attributed to competitive mortgage rates and the potential for long-term returns in the property market. Lenders have also been expanding their product offerings, providing landlords with a wider range of options to suit their investment strategies.

Strong Rental Yields

Rental yields remain a key driver of investor confidence, with many regions across the UK reporting steady or even increased returns. Whilst property prices in some areas have stabilised or dipped slightly, demand for rental properties continues to grow, particularly in urban centres and areas with strong employment markets. This sustained demand allows landlords to maintain healthy cash flow, making BTL investments an attractive proposition for many.

Low Arrears and Market Stability

Another positive indicator for the BTL sector is the low level of arrears reported across mortgage portfolios. This suggests that landlords are managing their finances effectively, even in a challenging economic climate. Additionally, the stability of the rental market has provided landlords with a reliable income stream, further reinforcing the resilience of the sector.

Whilst the BTL market faces ongoing regulatory changes and economic uncertainties, the latest figures paint a picture of sustained investor confidence. Borrowers considering entering the market or expanding their portfolios might wish to explore the current range of mortgage products available, whilst keeping a close eye on regional yield trends and market conditions.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The buy-to-let (BTL) market continues to demonstrate remarkable strength, with recent figures highlighting increased lending activity, robust yields, and low levels of arrears. These trends suggest sustained confidence among property investors, despite ongoing economic challenges and regulatory changes in the sector.

Growing Lending Activity

Recent data indicates a notable rise in BTL lending, reflecting renewed interest from both new and experienced landlords. This uptick in borrowing could be attributed to competitive mortgage rates and the potential for long-term returns in the property market. Lenders have also been expanding their product offerings, providing landlords with a wider range of options to suit their investment strategies.

Rental yields remain a key driver of investor confidence, with many regions across the UK reporting steady or even increased returns. Whilst property prices in some areas have stabilised or dipped slightly, demand for rental properties continues to grow, particularly in urban centres and areas with strong employment markets. This sustained demand allows landlords to maintain healthy cash flow, making BTL investments an attractive proposition for many.

Low Arrears and Market Stability

Another positive indicator for the BTL sector is the low level of arrears reported across mortgage portfolios. This suggests that landlords are managing their finances effectively, even in a challenging economic climate. Additionally, the stability of the rental market has provided landlords with a reliable income stream, further reinforcing the resilience of the sector.

Whilst the BTL market faces ongoing regulatory changes and economic uncertainties, the latest figures paint a picture of sustained investor confidence. Borrowers considering entering the market or expanding their portfolios might wish to explore the current range of mortgage products available, whilst keeping a close eye on regional yield trends and market conditions.
